It is understood that while God has the power to intervene, He "limits His power by not interfering" with human choices, allowing individuals the freedom to act according to their own will ([*The Bahá’í Writings: A Meta-ethical Excursion*](https://siftersearch.com/document/14605)). This perspective emphasizes that undesirable behaviors like "war, violence, and aggression are not inevitable" and can be changed through the exercise of free will ([*Strategies for Spiritualization*](https://siftersearch.com/document/14404)). Thus, free will is seen as a vital component of moral agency and personal responsibility.

Furthermore, God's Word is described as "sanctified from any property or substance," indicating that it transcends all known elements and is the "all-pervasive grace" from which all creation emanates ([*Tablets of Bahá’u’lláh*](https://siftersearch.com/document/8270)). response: The relationship between faith and reason in the Bahá'í teachings is one of harmony and complementarity.
